Curriculum → Application architecture and APIs → Service discovery and coordination

Lesson 3 of 5

Service discovery and coordination

Distributed services need a way to locate healthy instances, coordinate leaders, and react to topology changes without manual reconfiguration.

25-35 min Application architecture and APIs 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Discovery and coordination are invisible when done well, but they shape failover behavior, deployment safety, and the blast radius of outages.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Discovery patterns Client-side discovery gives more control to the caller2. Coordination patterns Prefer idempotent work over broad locking when possible3. Failure thinking Cache endpoint lists locally with expiration

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Discovery and coordination are invisible when done well, but they shape failover behavior, deployment safety, and the blast radius of outages. 2. Discovery patterns Client-side discovery gives more control to the caller3. Coordination patterns Prefer idempotent work over broad locking when possible4. Failure thinking Cache endpoint lists locally with expiration
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Discovery and coordination are invisible when done well, but they shape failover behavior, deployment safety, and the blast radius of outages.

What to say first

  • Explain how services find healthy peers.
  • Mention registries, health checks, and local caching.
  • Use coordination sparingly and prefer idempotency.
2

Study section

Discovery patterns

Clients can discover service instances directly, or a sidecar/proxy can hide the topology. Registries maintain health-aware instance lists.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Client-side discovery gives more control to the caller
  • Server-side discovery simplifies clients
  • Managed platforms often embed discovery in service meshes or load balancers
3

Study section

Coordination patterns

Leader election, leases, heartbeats, and distributed locks support operations that should not run everywhere at once.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Prefer idempotent work over broad locking when possible
  • Use leases to avoid stale leaders during partitions
  • Keep the coordination surface small
4

Study section

Failure thinking

Discovery systems can themselves become critical dependencies. Explain fallback behavior, cache staleness, and how callers handle partial topology information.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Cache endpoint lists locally with expiration
  • Use retries with jitter when instances churn
  • Observe registration freshness and health-check flapping

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain how services find healthy peers.
  • Mention registries, health checks, and local caching.
  • Use coordination sparingly and prefer idempotency.
  • Treat the discovery system as a failure domain with its own monitoring.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Centralizing every request through the discovery store.
  • Using distributed locks when commutative or idempotent work would do.
  • Ignoring stale endpoint lists and split-brain scenarios.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would a worker discover partitions it should process?
  • When is client-side discovery preferable?
  • What happens if discovery data is slightly stale?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Load balancing Networking, edge, and request routingIdempotency, retries, and back pressure Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Service discovery and coordination

Compare synchronous service hops against queued fan-out so you can explain where coupling shows up in production. Apply it to service discovery and coordination so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Diagnostics

Things to fix or tighten

  • Line 1: Start by adding at least one heading so the answer has a visible structure.
Preview

Explain the concept

Teach "Service discovery and coordination" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • How would a worker discover partitions it should process?
  • Discovery patterns
  • Coordination patterns
  • Explain how services find healthy peers.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs
